text: Linus Torvalds created the Linux kernel in 1991 in Helsinki, Finland.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989.
Steve Wozniak designed the Apple I personal computer in Cupertino in 1976.
Larry Page and Sergey Brin co-founded Google at Stanford University in 1998.
Guido van Rossum released the Python programming language in 1991 in Amsterdam.
Bjarne Stroustrup developed C++ at Bell Labs starting in 1979.
Marie Curie discovered polonium and radium in Paris in 1898, winning two Nobel Prizes in different sciences.
Albert Einstein published the theory of special relativity in 1905 while working at the Swiss Patent Office in Bern.
Isaac Newton formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation at Cambridge in 1687.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, proposing evolution by natural selection.
Rosalind Franklin produced the X-ray diffraction images that revealed DNA's double-helix structure at King's College London in 1952.
Niels Bohr developed the model of atomic structure with electron orbits in Copenhagen in 1913.
Thomas Edison patented the first commercially practical incandescent light bulb in Menlo Park in 1879.
Alexander Graham Bell received the patent for the telephone in 1876 in Boston.
Nikola Tesla designed the alternating current motor and demonstrated wireless power transmission in the late 1880s.
The Wright brothers achieved the first powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina in 1903.
Hedy Lamarr co-invented frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology, a foundation for modern Wi-Fi, in 1942.
Gutenberg developed the movable-type printing press in Mainz around 1440, enabling the spread of printed books.
Alan Turing proposed the universal computing machine model in his 1936 paper at Cambridge, founding theoretical computer science.
Ada Lovelace wrote the first computer algorithm for Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in 1843, making her the first programmer.
Leonhard Euler made foundational contributions to graph theory by solving the Seven Bridges of Konigsberg problem in 1736.
Carl Friedrich Gauss proved the fundamental theorem of algebra in 1799 in his doctoral dissertation at Helmstedt.
Emmy Noether published her theorem connecting symmetries and conservation laws in 1918 at the University of Gottingen.
Kurt Godel proved the incompleteness theorems in 1931 in Vienna, showing limits of formal mathematical systems.
Margaret Hamilton led the team that wrote the on-board flight software for the Apollo missions at MIT during the 1960s.
Grace Hopper developed the first compiler, A-0, at Remington Rand in 1952 and later contributed to COBOL.
Dennis Ritchie created the C programming language at Bell Labs in 1972 alongside Ken Thompson's work on Unix.
Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn designed the TCP/IP protocols that became the foundation of the modern Internet in the 1970s.
John von Neumann proposed the stored-program computer architecture in 1945 at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton.
Claude Shannon founded information theory with his 1948 paper at Bell Labs, defining the bit as a unit of information.
